claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit. 

To begin, I need to understand more about your child or children:

**How old is your child?** (If you have multiple children, let's start with one and we can assess each one)

The age categories are important because:
- **Under 16**: Children under 16 generally qualify automatically
- **16-19**: Teenagers in this age range may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally not eligible for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, please also let me know how many and their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. 

I have one child - Alex is 8 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since Alex is 8 years old, they fall into the "under 16" category. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to confirm a few more details about Alex:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old, I assume they're not working, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at all - they're only 8, so they're just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming that. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed in Alex's own name (not your benefits as the parent). 

Given that Alex is 8 years old and in primary school, I assume they're not claiming any benefits in their own right, but please confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name. It's just me as the parent who deals with any benefits.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, thank you for confirming. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old and in primary school, they wouldn't be in any type of apprenticeship program. Can you confirm Alex is not in any apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - Alex is just 8, so no, they're not in any apprenticeship. They're just doing regular primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status:

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you normally live in the UK, or does any of these other situations apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. I'm not in any of those special situations, just a regular UK resident.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You meet this requirement if ANY of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** (which you've already mentioned - Alex lives at home with you)
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - food, clothes, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned Alex lives at home with you in Manchester, it sounds like you live with Alex. Can you confirm this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me here in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about other potential claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another relative or guardian
- Anyone else who might have claimed Child Benefit for Alex

Please note: Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time. If someone else is claiming but you both agree that you should be the claimant instead, that can be arranged.

**Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to understand Alex's living situation in more detail:

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current living situation?**

- Is Alex currently in hospital (for an extended stay)?
- Is Alex in local authority care?
- Is Alex being fostered?
- Or is Alex living normally at home with you?

Since you mentioned Alex lives at home with you, I assume it's the last option, but please confirm which situation applies.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, Alex has been in the hospital for a while now - about 3 to 4 months. But I still regularly buy things for them - you know, their favorite snacks, magazines, clothes, that sort of thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for sharing that information. I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital. Let me check the rules for children in hospital.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. There are specific rules for children in hospital. Let me check the details:

**Has Alex's cumulative time in hospital exceeded 12 weeks (84 days)?**

You mentioned Alex has been in hospital for about 3 to 4 months. Let me clarify:

- **3 months would be approximately 12-13 weeks**
- **4 months would be approximately 16-17 weeks**

Important note: Child Benefit can continue for up to 12 weeks when a child is in hospital. If there have been multiple hospital stays with gaps of less than 28 days between them, those stays are added together.

**Can you tell me more precisely:**
1. How many weeks/days has Alex been in hospital continuously this time?
2. Has Alex had any previous hospital stays in recent months with short gaps (less than 28 days) between them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex has been in hospital for about 15 weeks continuously this time. There haven't been any other hospital stays recently - this is just one long stay.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that. So Alex has been in hospital for about 15 weeks continuously. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is important! Since Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, there's an exception that can apply:

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while they are in hospital?**

You mentioned earlier that you regularly buy things for Alex - favorite snacks, magazines, clothes, etc. This type of regular spending can allow Child Benefit to continue even after the 12-week period.

Can you confirm that you are indeed regularly spending money on Alex during their hospital stay?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - I regularly spend money on Alex. I buy their clothes, magazines, and their favorite snacks and food. I make sure they have what they need while they're in there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That regular spending is important and means you can continue to claim Child Benefit even though Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Almost there! I have one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local council paying for Alex's care or accommodation costs? This would be separate from NHS hospital care - I'm asking specifically about local council payments for accommodation or maintenance.

Are they paying towards Alex's costs, or not?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the local council isn't paying anything towards Alex's costs. It's just the NHS hospital care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! I now have all the information I need to provide you with your Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex. Even though Alex has been in hospital for longer than the standard 12-week limit, you qualify for an important **exception** because you regularly spend money on Alex during their hospital stay (buying clothes, magazines, snacks, and food).

### Why You're Eligible:

✅ Alex is 8 years old (under 16) - qualifying child  
✅ Alex is not working or receiving benefits in their own right  
✅ You live in the UK (Manchester) - meeting residency requirements  
✅ You live with and are responsible for Alex  
✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex  
✅ **You regularly spend money on Alex while they're in hospital** - this exception allows you to continue claiming beyond 12 weeks  
✅ The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply
5. **Payments** are typically made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**Apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

I hope Alex recovers soon. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16 and qualifies as a qualifying child; (2) Alex is not working and not receiving benefits in their own right; (3) Alex is not in any apprenticeship; (4) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements; (5) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for them; (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (7) Although Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (15 weeks), the claimant regularly spends money on Alex (clothes, magazines, snacks, food), which is an exception to the 12-week hospital rule; (8) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old child Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for 15 weeks (exceeding the standard 12-week limit), the claimant qualifies for the exception because they regularly spend money on Alex during the hospital stay. The claimant is a UK resident in Manchester, lives with and is responsible for Alex, and no other person is claiming Child Benefit for the child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16 and qualifies as a qualifying child; (2) Alex is not working and not receiving benefits in their own right; (3) Alex is not in any apprenticeship; (4) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements; (5) The claimant lives with Alex and is responsible for them; (6) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (7) Although Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (15 weeks), the claimant regularly spends money on Alex (clothes, magazines, snacks, food), which is an exception to the 12-week hospital rule; (8) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old child Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for 15 weeks (exceeding the standard 12-week limit), the claimant qualifies for the exception because they regularly spend money on Alex during the hospital stay. The claimant is a UK resident in Manchester, lives with and is responsible for Alex, and no other person is claiming Child Benefit for the child.
